problem_summary: 2016 MCM Problem D (titled "Space Debris" in some versions; this team treats it as the discrete-type ADR problem): evaluate whether there is a viable commercial opportunity for removing/mitigating space debris in low Earth orbit (LEO). The team reframes the open-ended question into a concrete business case: a single merged private firm that both sells collision insurance to satellite launchers AND removes hazardous debris. They then quantify feasibility via projected net income over a 15-year horizon (2016-2030), comparing land-based vs. space-based vs. combined removal alternatives, and add a policy-maker layer via game theory.
models_used: Model 1 - Time-Dependent Net-Income Evaluation Model: discrete year-by-year accounting recursion N_t = R_t - C_t with cumulative N_T = sum(N_i). Revenue R_t = n_t * w * alpha% (number of satellites x guaranteed value x actuarial commission rate). Cost C_t = C_1t (removal cost) + C_2t (expected compensation).

Cobb-Douglas utility function to value the per-collision compensation parameter delta. General form U = mu * x1^c * x2^d, reduced via monotonic transformation to U' = mu' * x1^k * x2^(1-k); they set k=0.5 to encode risk-aversion / diminishing marginal return, giving delta = w * alpha^(1/2) * mu.

Model 2 - Monte Carlo collision-probability model: a 1000x1000 binary (0/1) occupancy matrix representing a 2D radial cross-section of LEO. ~15% of cells seeded with debris using random seeds, with cell density drawn from a Gamma distribution split into 4 concentric ring layers (integrating the Gamma PDF per ring). Satellites randomly placed; a cell collision counts as a crash. Pr(t) = crashes / satellites launched, justified by the Law of Large Numbers (Pr(t) converges in probability to true b).

Gamma distribution as the spatial debris-density model: chosen because NASA's empirical debris-density-vs-altitude curve is visually analogous to a Gamma PDF; two parameters (shape alpha, rate beta) used to fit the layered radial profile.

Discrete-time simulation dynamics: yearly debris removal (Q_t updated by subtracting q_land + epsilon*q_space, gated by altitude work-range bands 600-800 km land / 800-1000 km space), debris growth (linear 5%/yr baseline, also a logarithmic growth fit), and satellite launches (start 110, +5% per year, fitted from Space-Track.org data).

Model 3 - Game Theory model: a 3x3 bimatrix game. Player 1 (satellite owner): uninsured / half-insured / fully-insured. Player 2 (insurer-remover firm): do nothing / remove some / remove considerably. Payoff entries populated from Models 1-2 outputs. Pure-strategy Nash Equilibria identified; a second payoff matrix (after new industrial standards) collapses the multiple NE to a single Pareto-improving equilibrium (half-insured, remove considerably).
Dummy/indicator variable rho in {0,1} to switch removal approaches on/off in the cost summation C_1t = sum over types of (p_t * q_t * rho).
key_techniques: Problem reframing: collapsing an open-ended 'is there a commercial opportunity?' prompt into a measurable net-income forecast by inventing a concrete actor (a merged insurer + debris-remover firm) - this turns a vague discrete/policy question into something computable.

Dimensional reduction with justification: explicitly 'smashing' the 3D spatial debris problem into a 2D radial cross-section, openly stating it is to avoid demanding 3D computation, and arguing the 2D circular model is representative and extensible.
Visual-analogy model selection: justifying the Gamma distribution by overlaying NASA's debris-density-vs-altitude curve against a Gamma PDF shape, rather than asserting it arbitrarily.
Monte Carlo + Law of Large Numbers: estimating collision probability by repeated random placement on the grid and invoking convergence to justify the empirical frequency as the true probability.

Three-scenario comparative structure: running the SAME simulation engine under land-only, space-only, and combined alternatives, then reading off both financial (profit curve) and physical (debris-left curve) outcomes to argue the combined option dominates.
Bringing economic theory (Cobb-Douglas utility, risk-aversion via k=0.5, actuarial commission) into a discrete engineering simulation to monetize risk.

Thorough sensitivity / 'what-if' analysis on the parameters they admit are guessed: debris growth rate (5/4/3/2%/log), rocket recycle lifetime (3/6/9/12), and the actuarial commission alpha (3/4/5/6%), each tied to a concrete decision recommendation.
Using game theory as a third lens to introduce the policy-maker as a distinct stakeholder and to deliver a clean 'Pareto improvement' policy recommendation.
why_it_won: "From a judge's view, this paper wins on framing, breadth, and decisiveness rather than mathematical depth. (1) It answers the actual question asked - it does not just simulate debris, it directly outputs a yes/no on commercial viability plus actionable numbers (alpha >= 4-5%, use the combined alternative, implement industrial standards). MCM judges reward papers that close the loop back to the prompt. (2) Three complementary models from genuinely different disciplines (accounting/finance, physics/probability, game theory) give a multi-perspective story that feels comprehensive without being scattered - each model feeds the next (Model 2's Pr(t) feeds Model 1's compensation cost; both feed Model 3's payoff matrix). (3) Strong, honest sensitivity analysis on exactly the parameters most open to criticism, which preempts judge skepticism and demonstrates the team understands which assumptions are load-bearing. (4) Every analysis ends in a concrete, quantified recommendation, so the paper reads as a decision tool, not an academic exercise. (5) Excellent narrative packaging - the Gravity movie hook, clear executive summary, and consistent dual-axis (profit vs. debris-remaining) story make it memorable and easy to judge favorably under time pressure."
section_highlights: "Structure is textbook-clean and judge-friendly: Summary Sheet (with a memorable Gravity hook) -> Executive Summary that already states all conclusions and headline sensitivity numbers -> Introduction/Background -> Existing Methods survey (land vs. space, real systems: Laser Broom, Huff-and-Puff/SpaDE, Snagging nets, Solar Sail) -> Terminologies + a Notation Table -> Model 1 -> Model 2 -> Results (the 3 alternatives) -> What-If/Sensitivity -> Game Theory -> Conclusions -> Strengths & Improvements -> References. Writing strengths: (a) the Executive Summary front-loads the verdict and the key numbers (e.g. 5%->4% growth cut reduces ending debris from 12302 to 9932), so a skimming judge gets everything; (b) consistent paired-metric storytelling - every result figure shows accumulated profit AND debris-remaining together, making the land-vs-space tradeoff legible at a glance; (c) assumptions are explicitly listed before each model and the notation table is centralized; (d) an honest, specific Strengths/Improvements section (cites the 2D simplification and arbitrary parameters as known weaknesses). Weak spots in the writing: noticeable ESL grammar slips and several lost symbols (alpha, k, Pr(t) render as '????' in places), and figure captions/numbering are inconsistent (Figure X reference, duplicated Figure 20/21)."
pitfalls_or_limitations: Many core parameters are openly admitted to be arbitrary 'educated guesses': the actuarial commission alpha, the Cobb-Douglas multiplier mu, the ~15% debris fill rate, and even k=0.5 - the model's headline conclusions (especially profit sign) hinge on these, which the team concedes in the sensitivity section.

The 15% grid-fill derivation is a hand-wavy area-ratio argument and is internally inconsistent with their own debris counts (they elsewhere cite ~12000+ debris while a 15% fill of a 1e6 cell grid implies ~150000).

2D reduction of an intrinsically 3D orbital-mechanics problem; debris is treated as static occupancy on a grid with no real orbital velocity, relative inclination, or Kessler-syndrome cascade dynamics - a same-cell overlap is called a 'crash' with no velocity/timing physics.
Ignores the time value of money (explicitly stated), which is a real flaw for a 15-year financial projection where billions are discounted.
Insurance commission is the ONLY revenue source, making the entire business case fragile to that single parameter - acknowledged as the dominant sensitivity.

Several figures appear to be schematic placeholders rather than real simulation output (multiple 'scatter plots' are just the line x=y from 100 to 1000, i.e. diagonal dummy data), so the visual evidence backing some claims is weak.
The Gamma distribution choice is justified only by visual shape resemblance to a NASA curve, with no goodness-of-fit test or parameter estimation shown.
Game-theory payoff numbers are asserted from prior models without showing the derivation, so the Nash-equilibrium analysis is hard to verify.

Conflates two different things both called 'alpha' (the Gamma shape parameter and the actuarial commission rate) and 'O-award type' labeling (paper is labeled Problem B/discrete in the corpus but is the space-debris ADR problem).
reusable_patterns: Reframe an open/qualitative prompt into a single computable scalar: pick a concrete decision-maker (here a merged firm) and a single benchmark metric (projected net income) so the whole essay drives toward a yes/no plus a number.

Layered, feeding models: build Model B (probability) so its output becomes an input to Model A (cost), then feed both into Model C (game-theoretic decision). This creates narrative coherence and lets each model justify the next.
Justify a distribution by overlaying the empirical data curve against the candidate PDF's shape - a fast, defensible way to motivate Gamma/Weibull/etc. without heavy fitting machinery.
Grid + Monte Carlo + Law of Large Numbers as a cheap, explainable way to estimate a collision/contact probability when closed-form geometry is hard; report convergence as the justification.

Run the identical simulation engine across 2-3 mutually exclusive strategies (A alone / B alone / A+B) and present a shared dual-axis tradeoff chart (cost-metric vs. effectiveness-metric) - makes the 'combined dominates' conclusion self-evident.
Pre-empt judge criticism by listing your guessed parameters explicitly, then sweeping each one in a dedicated sensitivity sub-section that ends in a numeric decision rule (e.g. 'set alpha >= 4%').

Use a small 3x3 bimatrix game to inject a third stakeholder (regulator) and produce a crisp 'Pareto improvement via new standards' policy recommendation - a clean way to add policy depth to a discrete/operations problem.
Front-load every conclusion and headline sensitivity number in the Executive Summary so a time-pressured judge can grade the paper from page one.
Use an indicator/dummy variable rho in {0,1} inside a cost summation to cleanly model 'turn this strategy on/off' across scenarios from one equation.
Always pair the economic/cost result with the physical/effectiveness result in the same figure so tradeoffs are unmistakable.
